statement: Samtök iðnaðarins (Confederation of Icelandic Industries, SI) has conducted periodic surveys of its members on EU membership. The surveys showed a decline in strong EU support: the proportion of members 'very much in favour' (mjög fylgjandi) of EU membership fell from approximately 17% in 2024 to approximately 10% in 2026. The proportion 'somewhat in favour' (frekar hlynntir) also declined from approximately 20% to approximately 15% over the same period. SI's leadership has interpreted this as reflecting growing concern among Icelandic industry about the practical terms of EU membership, particularly regarding regulatory burden and the Common Fisheries Policy.
source_name: Samtök iðnaðarins — Könnun meðal félagsmanna um ESB-aðild
caveats: SI member surveys represent the views of Icelandic industrial companies, not the general public. The survey methodology, sample size, and response rate may vary between years. SI's public position has been sceptical of EU membership, which may influence how results are presented. The exact question wording and response options should be verified against the original survey publications.

related_entries.statement: Gallup polls conducted in Iceland between 2022 and early 2026 show a gradual shift in public opinion on EU membership. Support for applying for EU membership rose from approximately 30% in 2022 to around 45% by late 2025, while opposition declined from roughly 50% to 40%. The remainder were undecided. The trend accelerated following the Althingi's decision in 2024 to hold a binding referendum.

Polling data consistently shows that support for EU membership in Iceland is higher among younger voters (18–34: ~55% in favour), urban residents in the capital region (~50%), and those with university education (~52%). Opposition is strongest among older voters (55+: ~50% opposed), rural residents (~55% opposed), and those employed in agriculture and fisheries (~65% opposed).
